name: Nettle
description: One of the first plants up in spring. Young leaves are prized in folk herbalism as a mineral-rich tonic — traditionally reached for when energy feels low after winter.

name: Dandelion
description: Every part is used. The root is roasted for a coffee-like tonic, while the leaf is bitter and traditionally taken to support digestion after a heavy winter diet.

name: Chickweed
description: A cooling, low-growing plant that carpets the garden in early spring. Its high moisture content makes it a favorite for soothing, cooling skin preparations.

name: Sweet Violet
description: Shade-loving and fleeting — the flowers only last a few weeks. Both leaf and blossom are gentle enough for daily use and lend a soft, floral note.

name: Lemon Balm
description: A member of the mint family that comes up fast and fragrant in spring. Bruise a leaf and the citrus scent is unmistakable — long used to take the edge off a stressful day.

name: Lavender
description: Harvested just as the buds open, before the oils peak and start to fade in the heat. The scent alone is often enough to slow a room down.

name: Chamomile
description: Small daisy-like flowers picked at the height of summer. A gentle, apple-scented herb reached for at the end of the day, or to settle a fussy stomach.

name: Elderflower
description: The creamy flower clusters bloom for a short window in early summer, well before the berries form in autumn. Traditionally taken at the first sign of a summer cold.

name: Calendula
description: Bright orange petals that keep blooming right through the hottest weeks. A shop staple for skin-soothing salves — reliable, cheerful, and easy to dry well.

name: Yarrow
description: A feathery-leaved plant found at the edge of meadows in midsummer. Long carried by herbalists for minor cuts and scrapes, hence the old nickname 'woundwort.'

name: Peppermint
description: Grows aggressively once summer heat sets in — most herbalists cut it back several times a season just to keep it contained. Cooling and reliably good for the stomach.

name: Elderberry
description: The same plant as summer's elderflower, now heavy with dark purple berries. Always cooked before use — one of the most requested immune-support ingredients in the shop.

name: Rosehip
description: The fruit left behind after the roses fade, sweetest once picked after the season's first light frost. A tart, vitamin-rich staple for the darker months ahead.

name: Echinacea
description: The root is dug once the flowers have died back and the plant's energy has moved underground for winter — considered the most potent part of the plant.

name: Hawthorn
description: Deep red berries that ripen on hedgerows in early autumn. A gentle, long-used herb for supporting the heart, both literally and in the old folk sense.

name: Garden Sage
description: Cut back hard before the first frost, both to preserve the harvest and to keep the plant healthy through winter. A classic remedy for sore, scratchy throats.

name: Ginger
description: Doesn't grow locally, so the shop relies on cured, stored root through winter. Warming and a first reach for anyone walking in cold and a little queasy.

name: Cinnamon
description: Cured bark, stored in coils in the back pantry. Used sparingly — a little goes a long way toward warming both a tea blend and a cold room.

name: Licorice Root
description: Naturally sweet dried root, kept on hand through the cold months. Often blended into throat and cough teas to round out sharper, more bitter herbs.

name: Pine
description: Evergreen, so it's one of the only fresh greens to gather in the dead of winter. The needles carry a bright, resinous scent and a good dose of vitamin C.

name: Clove
description: Dried flower buds, stored whole in glass to keep their oil from fading. A small, potent ingredient — used a few buds at a time, never by the handful.
